column6: message
In addition to the current curriculum? What would it be replacing?

How old are you? I had computer class throughout my elementary education. First grade through 6th. There would be one or two classes a week. And it would cycle between P.E., Art, Music and Computer Lab throughout the week. It was common in the early 2000s to have a computer class. Now kids have such an easy access to a computer a basic computer literacy class isn't exactly helpful.

"are you kidding me? You think you're some kind of expert on education just because you had computer class in elementary school? Newsflash, it's not like you're still using the same computers from the 90s. And by the way, I'm 22, not some ancient age. And yeah, basic computer literacy is still super relevant, especially for a generation that's growing up with social media and online gaming. You're just mad because you didn't have to deal with the struggle of learning how to use a computer in the first place."
@jovial-term, @diachronic-clarinet, let's keep the discussion civil.

We had to actually learn how to use a typewriter and do math problems by hand. And yeah, I'm mad that kids today don't have to deal with that, but that doesn't mean they're not still learning the basics. And by the way, 22 is not ancient, but you're still young and you can learn to appreciate the value of basic computer literacy. You're just too caught up in your own generation's technology to see the bigger picture."
@diachronic-clarinet, @tough-pergola, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic.

column6:
But we should have a draft.  We should have a small standing army ready to train troops as needed, but when we go to war, we should have a draft pulling from all individuals between 18 and 25, no excuses allowed. 

This way, war is taken very, very seriously.

I previously was very much on the same page as you. I was then pointed toward another viewpoint. While you or I may not agree with the military's actions quite often, I think we can agree that it is necessary for our countries to have standing armies in case defense is needed. Endlessly praising those who willingly enter the military keeps a steady flow of cocky, overly patriotic young people doing so. This prevents the need for a draft to be instated.  While it may be silly to us, it is ultimately helpful and potentially life saving to those who are not trigger happy military enthusiasts.

"Are you kidding me, @ebony-vocoder? You're saying that a draft is necessary because it keeps 'cocky, overly patriotic young people' in the military? That's just a nice way of saying it keeps the dumb jocks in the military. Newsflash: most of the time, it's the smart, educated people who end up getting drafted, not the jocks. And what about all the people who are too smart for the military? Should we just draft them too? And by the way, 'potentially life saving'? You sound like a total government drone. What's next, 'the draft is necessary for the greater good'?"

column6:
I see it more with the asian characters (Rogue One and TLJ specifically - where you have these asian characters who are kind of cookie cutter heroic).  But you are right the default position shouldn't be just casting white.

In a movie set in America today I think if you are going to have a Black character (who isn't a token) you need to somehow deal with the racial aspect.  It doesn't have to be huge, but its a part of black americans' lives and so it should be reflected in the character.  

In science fiction/fantasy?  Its harder because that social context is different but at the same time science fiction/fantasy are supposed to adopt those settings because it is allegorical.  Star Trek shouted a message about race from the rooftops just by inserting a black character. 

I think the sloppy tokenism colors my perception of whether or not Finn is a token character.  However... do you feel the decision for him to be black was made specifically for the purpose of having a black character by a board of executives - or do you think it was just the organic choice of the writer / casting agent based on pure talent?

So you brought up Token and it got me thinking: how is, say, Finn a token? 

I can't think of any explanation that isn't super racist and doesn't boil down to "all major characters in movies should be white unless there is a compelling reason for them not to be."

"Are you kidding me? You're defending Finn's character because he's a black character? That's not about the character being well-written or well-cast, that's about the tokenism that's still prevalent in this industry. If Finn was a white character, you'd be the first one to complain about how he's a 'cookie cutter' hero. Get over it."
@current-value, @frayed-ranch, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic.

column6:
I'm going to challenge the **any** part of your argument; technically, there are _some_ types of firearms that we could probably effectively ban and remove if we were so inclined.

Fully-automatic firearms have been illegal to produce and sell to civilians since 1986.  It is still legal to own them and do person to person sales, but it requires the weapon to be licenced and registered with the ATF.  It is probably the only class of weapon where we _do_ have the registers necessary to locate these weapons if we wanted to confiscate them.

Now, this wouldn't make a dent in gun crime since these guns are so rare and pretty much never used in criminal activities.  Still, a ban on such weapons _could_ be successful, if the goal was just to ban them.

"Are you kidding me, @frayed-ranch? You're trying to spin this into a 'we could just ban them' situation? That's not the point. The point is that these guns are still out there, and we can't even track them. You're not addressing the issue of gun crime, you're just nitpicking about the logistics. And by the way, just because they're rare doesn't mean they're not used in crimes. We need to focus on the bigger picture here, not just some technicality about licensing and registration."

\>  seizing the means of production (the central tennet of socialism at least according to my understanding of the communist manifesto). 

Do the working classes control the means of production or does the government? And the communist manifesto doesn't explain Marxism or socialism as a theory in any depth. Venezuala has messed up a lot and for propaganda reasons it likes to blame global capitalism and act as if they're the bastion of workers revolution, but it needs to be understood that this isn't the reality. The workers don't control production. It's a government with socialist aspirations, but not socialism.
Yeah Everytime it doesn't work it's "not socialism". Explain to me how the workers are going to own the means of production without it being through the government please.

{"role": "user", "content": "User threadbare-assignment, I'm surprised you're so quick to dismiss the idea of workers owning the means of production. You're basically saying that the only way for socialism to work is through a government-controlled system, which is exactly what we're trying to get away from. The whole point of socialism is to give the people a say in how their own economy is run, not to have some faceless government telling them what to do. And by the way, Venezuela's problems are a perfect example of what happens when a government tries to impose socialism without understanding the underlying principles. It's not about blaming capitalism, it's about understanding that socialism requires a different approach."}

column6:
I was one of those kids.  My grandparents all built up their lives through honest hard work and good planning from almost nothing.  My parents were responsible enough to build on what their parents had done and not lose it all.

I have grown up ashamed of the situation I was born into.  I was and disconnected from my past because I believed rich people were evil, because everyone was always saying they were.  I found it difficult to connect with my peers because of our different financial situations.  I found it difficult to cope when I realized I wasn't better and my position made me uncomfortable.  Nobody talks about my problems or has compassion for them.  I've been made fun of my whole life because I was a "trust funder", instead of being supported in becoming the best I could be for my people and encouraged to take the social responsibility of what I'd been given seriously.

I can't speak for other families, but in general I've come to believe rich people are not evil, they are often just financially very responsible.

Do you have zero respect or compassion for me?  Do you believe I should be neglected?

If nobody deserves special privileges because their ancestors had bad luck, why do some deserve special contempt because their ancestors had good luck?

> but to say that they deserve special privileges because their ancestors had bad luck is asinine

So what about the white kids with rich parents who don't have to work a day in their life? They inherit property, land, have top dollar educations? Are they surviving because they're the "fittest"?
column18: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column6: "Are you kidding me? You're still spewing that nonsense about 'fittest' and'surviving'? That's just a euphemism for 'I'm better than you' and 'you're not good enough'. Newsflash: just because someone's parents were successful doesn't mean they're inherently 'better' than others. And by the way, I'm not 'neglecting' anyone, I'm just pointing out the hypocrisy of your privileged attitude. And as for deserving special privileges, it's not about luck, it's about hard work and opportunity. You can't just dismiss the fact that some people have been given a head start in life, and then act like you're some kind of victim because of it. Get over yourself."
@threadbare-assignment, your response is appreciated. However, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic.

column6: White people speaking proper English is another topic.  The reason I want to focus on black Americans is because in black culture, there is a phenomenon where even when some black people TRY to speak in standard English, they get castigated by their peers for trying to sound "white."  I'm trying to argue that this is a bad thing.

> White people speaking proper English is another topic.

It really isn't.  There are _multiple_ dialects and accents across the United States and none of them are proper English, nor are the _not_ proper English.  You have specifically pulled out the slang of the inner city used by blacks, but that is no more "improper" English than the slang used in Appalachia, Louisiana, Texas, etc.  

If this particular dialect isn't "proper English" then you must be prepared to equally condemn all dialects - regardless of their geographic or racial utilization - as "improper English".  Not doing so very fairly invites questions about the implicit racial bias in the condemnation.

column6: "Vibrato-cone, I'm not trying to be racist, I'm just saying that when I hear someone speaking'standard' English, I assume it's because they're trying to fit in or be accepted by the majority culture. It's not about condemning certain dialects, it's about the fact that black Americans are often held to a higher standard of language proficiency than other groups. I'm not saying it's fair, but it's a reality. And by the way, just because there are multiple dialects doesn't mean they're all equal. Some are more widely accepted than others."
